title: Gyros — pork
subtitle: Home-style sliced pork gyros in pitta with tzatziki
excerpt: Pork shoulder marinated in warm spices, olive oil, and lemon, then roasted or pan-fried until caramelised and sliced thin. Served in warm pitta with a quick tzatziki of Greek yoghurt, cucumber, and dill, topped with tomato and red onion.
sourceNotes: Gyros (γύρος, meaning 'turn') refers to the rotating spit of compressed meat. The home version adapts the technique by roasting a whole piece of spiced pork and slicing it thin — the same flavour profile without the vertical rotisserie. Greek gyros is closely related to Turkish döner kebap; both traditions derive from the Ottoman vertical spit-roasting tradition of the nineteenth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pork can marinate overnight and be roasted ahead; slice to order and warm through in a hot pan.

content.text: The key difference between home gyros and souvlaki is the spice profile. Gyros uses cumin, paprika, and oregano together in a paste; souvlaki uses just lemon and oregano. The meat is also treated differently: roasted as a whole piece and sliced thin after resting, rather than cubed and skewered. The slices pick up additional char when briefly returned to a hot pan before serving.

The tzatziki can be made while the pork roasts. Let it sit for at least thirty minutes so the cucumber releases its water through the salt and the garlic flavour permeates the yoghurt. Squeeze the cucumber hard before stirring in — wet tzatziki falls out of the pitta.

items.prepNote: grated, salted, and squeezed very dry

content.text: Mix the {{oregano-dried}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{garlic}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, salt, and pepper into a paste. Rub all over the {{pork-shoulder}}. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Place the pork in a roasting tin and roast for 20 minutes until the outside is coloured. Lower to 170°C fan and roast for a further 60 to 70 minutes until the internal temperature reaches 72°C and the outside is dark and caramelised. Rest for 10 minutes, then slice very thin across the grain.

Grate the {{cucumber}}, toss with a pinch of salt, and leave in a colander for 20 minutes. Squeeze very hard to remove all liquid. Mix with the {{greek-yoghurt}}, {{garlic}} clove, {{dill}}, and {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Season and refrigerate until needed.

Heat a dry frying pan over high heat. Quickly fry the pork slices in batches for 1 to 2 minutes until the edges char. Warm the {{pitta-bread}} in the same pan. Spread generously with tzatziki, add the charred pork slices, {{tomato}}, and {{red-onion}}. Squeeze lemon over and serve immediately.

items.cause: Overcooked past 72°C internal, or rested without covering
items.fix: Use a thermometer; rest loosely covered in foil for at least 10 minutes; slicing thin helps with any slight dryness

items.symptom: Tzatziki is watery and slides out of the pitta
items.fix: Salt, drain, then squeeze by hand as hard as possible; the cucumber holds a surprising amount of water
A minimum of 4 hours; overnight is strongly preferable for the spices to penetrate the meat

items.symptom: No char in the pan finish
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too much pork at once
items.fix: The pan must be very hot and completely dry; work in small batches so the pork sears rather than steams

content.text: Gyros is the other great Greek street food alongside souvlaki, and the distinction matters to Greeks even when it confuses everyone else. The vertical rotating spit produces a compressed, layered meat that is shaved thin to order; the home version approximates this by roasting and slicing. In Athens, every neighbourhood has a gyradiko, open late and serving pitta gyros with chips tucked inside. Pork gyros dominates in central and southern Greece; the north uses a mixture of pork and chicken. The tzatziki that accompanies it is the standard Greek dip: yoghurt, cucumber, garlic, and dill, made correctly only when the cucumber is squeezed completely dry.
